seo.title: Semantic Kernel Guardrails for Legal
seo.description: Compliance guide for Semantic Kernel agents in legal. Meet Attorney-client privilege protections, Bar association ethics rules requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Legal teams using Semantic Kernel face heightened risks due to the framework's plugin chaining, planners, and memory systems, which can produce cascading errors, invalid plans, hallucinated interactions, or data contamination leading to breaches of attorney-client privilege or fabricated case citations. The Mata v. Avianca precedent underscores how AI-generated hallucinations can result in court sanctions and malpractice suits. Vex provides essential guardrails by integrating as a kernel filter to validate outputs against legal compliance standards.

complianceRequirements.description: All client communications and data processed by AI must remain confidential and not be logged, stored, or used externally without explicit consent.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex filters redact or block privileged data in Semantic Kernel function results, ensuring no leakage during plugin chaining or memory recall.

complianceRequirements.description: AI-generated legal advice must be clearly labeled as such and require licensed attorney review before use.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex adds compliance tags and review flags to planner outputs, preventing unverified content from reaching end-users.

complianceRequirements.description: Citations must reference real, verifiable cases; fabricated references violate filing rules.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex validates case citations in real-time against legal databases, blocking hallucinated references from execution plans.

complianceRequirements.description: AI cannot contaminate or alter discovery data across plugins; all outputs must preserve chain of custody.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex traces and sanitizes cross-plugin data flows, preventing contamination in Semantic Kernel pipelines.

industryRisks.name: Plugin chaining producing cascading errors
industryRisks.description: Errors in one legal research plugin propagate through drafting plugins, generating invalid contracts.
industryRisks.example: A flawed case search leads to incorrect privilege waiver language in a contract plugin.

industryRisks.name: Planner generating invalid execution plans
industryRisks.description: Planners sequence plugins to output unverified legal memos without attorney review flags.
industryRisks.example: Plan skips citation validation, filing brief with Mata v. Avianca-style hallucination.

industryRisks.name: Memory recall hallucinating past interactions
industryRisks.description: Kernel memory fabricates prior client consultations, breaching privilege by inventing details.
industryRisks.example: AI recalls non-existent settlement discussion, advising client on false premises.

industryRisks.name: Cross-plugin data contamination
industryRisks.description: Discovery data from one plugin leaks into unrelated client matter plugins.
industryRisks.example: eDiscovery docs from Case A contaminate privilege analysis for Case B.

implementation.title: Add Vex Filter to Kernel Function Results (Python)
implementation.description: Install via `pip install semantic-kernel vex-sdk` and register Vex as a post-invocation filter to validate legal outputs for privilege and citations.
implementation.code:
import semantic_kernel as sk
from vex_sdk import VexValidator

kernel = sk.Kernel()

# Legal compliance validator
legal_validator = VexValidator(
    policy_id="legal-compliance",
    rules=[
        "redact_privileged_data",
        "validate_case_citations"
    ]
)

# Add as function result filter
@kernel.register_function(
    plugin_name="LegalResearch",
    function_name="research_case"
)
def research_case(query: str) -> str:
    # Simulated plugin logic
    result = f"Case: {query} - Hallucinated details"
    validated = legal_validator.validate(result)
    return validated

# Usage
result = kernel.invoke("LegalResearch", "research_case", query="Mata v. Avianca")

implementation.title: TypeScript Kernel Filter for Planner Outputs
implementation.description: Integrate Vex to intercept Semantic Kernel planner plans, blocking invalid sequences that skip attorney review.

implementation.title: Python Memory Recall Sanitization
implementation.description: Protect against hallucinated memory recall by filtering Semantic Kernel chat history for confidential data.
implementation.code:
import semantic_kernel as sk
from vex_sdk import VexValidator

kernel = sk.Kernel()
chat_service = sk.ChatCompletion(
    service_id="gpt-4",
    service_name="openai"
)

legal_filter = VexValidator(
    policy_id="privilege-protection",
    rules=["sanitize_memory_recall", "detect_hallucinations"]
)

chat_history = kernel.add_chat_service("openai", chat_service)

async def safe_recall(prompt: str):
    history = await chat_history.get_chat_history_async()
    sanitized = legal_filter.validate(str(history))
    response = await kernel.invoke_prompt(prompt + sanitized.output)
    return response

# Invoke
result = await safe_recall("Recall prior client discussion on settlement.")

caseStudyScenario.heading: Semantic Kernel in Legal: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario: A mid-sized law firm deploys Semantic Kernel to streamline eDiscovery and brief drafting. Plugins handle case research, privilege analysis, and document generation, orchestrated by the kernel's planner. During a high-stakes merger case, the research plugin pulls potentially privileged emails, which the planner chains into a privilege memo plugin. However, memory recall hallucinates a prior client interaction, contaminating the output with fabricated settlement details. The planner generates an execution plan skipping citation verification, producing a brief with unverifiable case references resembling Mata v. Avianca. Without guardrails, this flows to court filing, risking sanctions.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Integrating Vex as a kernel filter catches the issues: privilege data is redacted, hallucinated memory is blocked, citations are validated against real databases, and the plan is flagged for attorney review. The brief is corrected before filing, averting malpractice exposure. The firm processes 40% more cases compliantly, with zero compliance violations post-deployment.
